District Manual – Soil Health Card PREFACE The purpose of this Development Manual for Soil Health Card (SHC) Scheme is to create an enabling mechanism for improved implementation of the Centrally Sponsored Scheme (CSS) at the cutting edge, leading to enhanced outcomes in nature and extent. Accordingly, it would act as guide for implementation by the District Collector and key District-level functionaries, enable quick learning about the Scheme, implementation modalities, roles and responsibilities of various functionaries as well as stakeholders This Manual is prepared with inputs from combination of sources, such as review of extant scheme guidelines, circulars and amendments issued by the Department of Agriculture, Cooperation & Farmers Welfare (DAC & FW), Government of India from time to time and recently updated in February 2017 and discussions with the key personnel involved in implementation of the Scheme. For greater direction, the guidelines cited must be referred to along with the SHC website (http://www.soilhhealth.dac.gov.in/) for guidance and clarification on implementation from time to time.

District Manual – Soil Health Card . Soil Health Card Scheme .. Introduction Sustaining agricultural productivity depends on quality and availability of natural resources like soil, water, etc. Intensive agriculture with growth in food grain production, use of varieties of seeds, usage of fertilizers with the existing Nitrogen, Phosphorus & Potassium consumption ratio is skewed from :: (2012-13) as against preferred ratio of ::. Fertilizer recommendations rarely matches soil fertility need, and usage of fertilizers differs from place to place. .. About the Scheme Soil health card scheme is one of the flagship programmes of Government of India that was launched in February 2015. Schemes managed by Integrated Management (INM) Division in the Ministry of Agriculture Cooperation and Farmers’ Welfare (AC&FW), Government of India (GoI). This scheme was launched to help farmers to know their soil health condition as represented by 12 important soil parameters (viz. Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Potassium, pH, EC, Organic Carbon, Sulphur, Zinc, Boron, Iron, Manganese and Copper) and follow management practices accordingly. Under this scheme, soil samples collected from different locations are analyzed in the Soil Testing Labs (STL) as per the norms provided in the scheme’ operational guidelines. The results are uploaded in the national Soil Health Card portal which has been developed for registration of soil samples, recording test results of soil samples and generation of Soil Health Cards (SHCs) along with fertilizer recommendations besides an information module for monitoring progress. To issue Soil Health Cards (SHCs) to all 14 Crore holdings in the country about .53 Crore samples are to be analyzed. The cycle is proposed to be implemented in two years, with target of Crore samples for the year 2015-16 and .53 Crore samples in the year 2016-17. .. Objectives  To issue soil health cards every two years to all farmers, so as to provide basis to address nutrient deficiencies in fertilization practices.  To strengthen functioning of Soil Testing Laboratories (STLs) through capacity building, involvement of agriculture students and effective linkage with Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) / State Agricultural Universities (SAUs).  To diagnose soil fertility related constraints with standardized procedures for sampling uniformly across states and analysis and design taluka / block level fertilizer recommendations in targeted districts.

District Manual – Soil Health Card .. Process Flow District Annual Action Plans are prepared by the Joint Director (Agriculture) with inputs from Tehsil or Mandal Agriculture Officers. The Annual Action Plan shall include broad components such as number of soil samples to be drawn, number of villages to be covered; details of training programmes to increase the capacities of the field staff; financial assistance to be provided for micronutrients and soil ameliorations, etc. Preparation of Soil Sample Feild data entry District Annual Collection as per into SHC Web Action Plan given norms Portal Soil Health Card Entry of Test Results Printing, & relevant Distribution & Soil Test Analysis recommendations awareness to into SHC Portal farmers Under Soil Health Management, establishment of soil testing labs in one of the components which are mainly located at district / block level. Therefore, Village Level Soil Testing Projects (VLSTP) will be established with the objective of promoting location as well as crop specific sustainable soil health management, employment generation for rural youth and to improve timelines in analysis of soil samples, to introduce the Single Window approach from collection to issue of SHC so as to minimize delays and maximize convenience to farmers and finally provide soil testing facility to farmers at their door- step. . Roles & responsibilities of District Level Functionaries The success of the programme is critically dependent on timely and efficient discharge of the roles and responsibilities of district level functionaries. The office of JD ()/DD () are identified as the district nodal agency. . Roles and Responsibilities, Chairman, DLEC Table : Role of District Collector Role Responsibilities  To provide inputs on the proposed plan prepared by the district agriculture department Planning  To provide inputs and direction on ICT& IEC mechanism for better implementation of the scheme  Fund mobilization by coordinating with Central / State officials for Implementation timely release of funds of the scheme

District Manual – Soil Health Card Role Responsibilities  To ensure adequate infrastructure is available for implementation of the scheme  To ensure that linkages with ICAR/KVK & SAU are effective in implementation of the programme  To monitor & evaluate the progress of the Scheme against the targets assigned Monitoring  To monitor the financial assistance provided on the package of soil nutrients is timely transferred . Roles and Responsibilities of Other District Officials Table : Role of District Agriculture Officer Role Responsibilities  Prepare District Annual Action Plan (AAP) for consideration of DLEC as per given guidelines PLANNING  Target setting for Divisions/Blocks Officers as per District Annual Action Plan Provision of Infrastructure for the Scheme  Establish Mini Soil Testing Labs at Village level3 by Village Entrepreneurs for analyzing soil samples  Procure Geo Positioning Systems (GPS) instruments, Mini Soil Testing Labs/Kits in coordination with State Level Officers for analyzing soil samples  For analyzing soil samples during heavy work load, take the help of Agriculture University labs, involve private agencies through tender system Human Resource Management  Deploy Assistant Directors in the division level for supervision  Ensure required line staff is arranged in the District, if staff is not IMPLEMENTATION sufficient, take help of students of SAU / Science College for Soil Sample Collection & Analysis Financial Assistance and Fund Management  Disburse funds to AD () to pay honorarium to field staff as per cost norms provided in the Scheme. Refer to annexure for honorarium details  Disburse funds to AD () for expenditure on Trainings & Field demonstrations. (Refer Annexure VI for fund details on Trainings & Demonstrations)  Provide financial assistance to farmers to apply corrective measures for nutrient deficiencies  Encourage rural youth, Village Level Soil Testing Project (VLSTP)

District Manual – Soil Health Card Role Responsibilities with maximum project cost of Rs. Lakhs. Refer Annexure IV for VLSTP cost norms  Coordinate and arrange for the technical inputs of ICAR & KVK to field level staff for smooth implementation and in time completion of the Scheme targets  Arrange Trainings on soil sample collection, analysis to line staff, students in coordination with ICAR / KVK / SAUs etc.  Bring awareness to farmers on the scheme and its benefits by arranging workshops, public meetings in coordination with line staff / PRIs / ICAR / KVKs etc.  Bring in awareness on Information Communication Technology (ICT) mechanisms up to grass-root level like on-line delivery of SHC to farmers, status of the samples taken etc. Farmers are given Unique ID. through which they can access the SHC portal and print their Soil Health Cards  Ensuring that targets of soil sample collection, analysis and SHC distribution are achieved as per AAP. MONITORING,  Verify and monitor the utilization of funds at the district level and EVALUATION AND report the same to State level functionaries REPORTING  Evaluate MIS reports submitted by divisional / block officers and submit to State. Table : Role of Assistant Director (Agriculture) and Soil Testing Lab (STL) head Role Responsibilities  Target setting as per District Annual Action Plan for PLANNING Cluster/Village Officers Provision of Infrastructure for the Scheme  Provide GPS instruments to Field level officers  Arrange required infrastructure at Cluster & Mandal level offices Human Resource Management  Encourage Village youth for arranging Mini Soil Testing Labs at the village level for analyzing soil samples in coordination with District Officials  Deploy Agriculture Extension Officers in every cluster for soil IMPLEMENTATION sample collection & analysis in Mini Soil Testing Labs Printing & Distribution of SHC  SHC are to be printed and issued to field staff for distribution to farmers by AD() - STL  Field staff to distribute SHC to farmers in time by AD() – Divisional Head Financial Assistance & Fund Management  Pay honorarium to the field level staff for soil sample collection and analysis of the soil

District Manual – Soil Health Card Role Responsibilities  Draw funds to pay honorarium to field staff and for other Scheme expenditure  Educate farmers on Soil Nutrition Management and facilitate in getting financial assistance  Disburse the financial assistance to SHGs / FPO etc. or Village Entrepreneurs for setting up Mini Soil Testing Labs.  Draw the funds provided under the scheme for disbursal for different purposes under the scheme Coordination, Capacity Building & IEC  Coordinate with district officials and arrange for the technical inputs of ICAR & KVK to field level staff in soil sample collection and analysis  Arrange Trainings on soil sample collection, analysis to line staff, students in coordination with ICAR / KVK / SAUs etc.  Provide awareness to farmers on benefits of soil nutrition management by arranging trainings and awareness programs  Monitor progress of the Scheme and ensure that target of SHC distribution is achieved as per AAP  Monitor the sample collection to ensure that required technical methods are followed while collecting samples by AD() – Divisional Head  Ensure the soil samples submitted to Soil Testing Lab are analyzed MONITORING, in time by AD() – STL EVALUATION &  Verify the soil test results randomly by AD() – STL REPORTING  Ensure that proper recommendations are given to all analyzed soil samples by AD() - STL  To ensure, test results and recommendations are uploaded in SHC portal in time by AD() - STL  Collect, evaluate & consolidate the MIS reports of all his/her mandals submit the same to DAO Table : Role of Agriculture Extension Officer (Village / Cluster Level) / Agriculture Officer (Mandal / STL) Students (SAU / Colleges) Role Responsibilities Soil Sample Collection  Collect soil samples4 from the fields as per following instructions . In irrigated areas, large, medium and semi-medium holdings5 will be sampled holding-wise. In case of marginal and small holdings sampling in . ha. Grid will be IMPLEMENTATION followed. ii. In rain-fed areas, all the large holdings will be sampled holding-wise and in case of medium, semi-medium, small & marginal holdings will be sampled and tested in 10 ha. Grid. 10

District Manual – Soil Health Card Role Responsibilities iii. Soil sample should be collected to maximum depth of 15 cm with spade/kassi/kurpi etc. iv. The ideal time for collection of soil samples is between harvests of one crop and sowing / planting of other crop, when fields are vacant. . Details of the farmer, land, soil, GPS coordinates etc. have to be essentially recorded in prescribed statement. Soil Analysis  Enter details of Soil sample data into SHC portal  Ensure that the received soil samples are properly sealed and tagged with required information slips  Complete soil analysis within weeks of receipt of samples in the Soil Testing Labs  Processes soil samples following as per the prescribed standard procedures and analysed for various parameters namely pH, Electrical Conductivity (EC), Organic Carbon (OC), and available , , , and micronutrients  Identify and publish Major / Secondary / Micro nutrient details in the Soil Health Card  Based on soil nutrient status, recommend appropriate usage of FYM, Urea, DAP, MOP etc. basing on season and crop  Test results & recommendations are to be uploaded in SHC portal  Printed Soil Health Cards issued at STLs are to be distributed to farmers concerned. For sample SHC format refer to Annexure III  Honorarium to field staff as per cost norms provided in the Scheme.  Attend trainings on soil sample collection / soil analysis COORDINATION,  To provide awareness to farmers on benefits of soil nutrition CAPACITY management BUILDING, IEC &  To announce soil test results to farmers in Gram Sabhas and ICT MECHANISMS suggest corrective measures on soil nutrition (if any)  Provide awareness to farmers . Tentative timelines for issuing Soil Health Cards (SHC) # Activity Timeline for completion Sample Collection & Farmer Registration on SHC Portal to 10 days Analysis of Soil samples 11 to 20 days Develop Soil nutrition recommendations and upload the 21 to 25 days same in the SHC portal Printing & Distribution of Soil Health Cards 26 to 30 days 11
